content: I'm thinking about adding proactive recall to my Second Brain app. The main challenge is deciding when to interrupt the user without being annoying

Good problem — it's really about optimizing helpfulness vs. interruption cost. A short framework and a few concrete heuristics to try:

1) Clarify goals
- What’s the primary purpose of proactive recall? (e.g., memory retention, habit formation, reducing future search time)
- Which user states is interruption most harmful? (deep work, meetings, driving)

2) Signal should match intent
- Low-cost cues when interrupting risk is high: subtle indicators (badge, dock icon, status bar, passive card) that the user can check at will.
- Higher-cost interruptions (modal, push, sound) only when expected utility is high.

3) Score interruption value = Benefit − Cost
- Benefit = importance of item * decay risk * user interest/priority.
  - importance: user-set or derived from frequency, tags, deadlines.
  - decay risk: time since last review, repetition schedule (spaced repetition).
  - interest: inferred from past dismissals/engagement or explicit preferences.
- Cost = current interruptibility (context) * annoyance propensity.
  - interruptibility: calendar status, Do Not Disturb, active app, battery, location.
  - annoyance propensity: how often user has dismissed recent prompts, time of day.

4) Context sensing (low friction)
- Use calendar and focus/Do Not Disturb to detect high-cost times.
- Detect active full-screen apps, video/audio playing, driving state (if mobile).
- Track short-term dismiss rate: if user dismisses >X prompts in Y minutes, reduce frequency.

5) Delivery strategy (graduated)
- Passive: queue of suggested recalls shown in sidebar or daily summary.
- Gentle nudge: timed notification with snooze options, or glanceable widget.
- Immediate interrupt: only when score exceeds high threshold (e.g., important + urgent).

6) Personalization & control
- Let users pick aggressiveness levels (gentle, balanced, pushy) and set quiet hours.
- Offer a “teaching” period: try heuristics, then ask a quick question: “Was this interruption helpful?” Use answers to tune thresholds.
- Provide simple commands: pause for X hours, remind later, don’t remind about this topic.

7) Adaptation rules to start with (example defaults)
- No push notifications during calendar events marked Busy or when Do Not Disturb is on.
- If user dismissed >3 prompts within 24 hours, reduce frequency by 50% for 48 hours.
- Only use interruptive notification if Value Score > 0.8 (normalized); otherwise add to passive list.
- Prefer morning or early evening for recall nudges; avoid nights.

8) Metrics to measure and iterate
- Engagement rate (opened / notified), dismiss rate, snooze rate, retention improvement for items reviewed, user satisfaction surveys.
- A/B test thresholds and delivery channels.

One concrete experiment to run now
- Implement a two-channel flow: daily passive digest + up to 2 push nudges per day. Score items with a simple benefit−cost model (benefit = spaced-rep urgency * user-set priority; cost = calendar busy OR DND). After 2 weeks, measure dismiss/engagement and ask users one quick in-app question about annoyance. Use that to refine thresholds.

Question for you: which cost signals can your app already read (calendar, DND, active window, location)? That determines how sophisticated your interruption model can be quickly.
I think a strict threshold on the reranker score is the best gate. If it's above 0.8, surface it as a side-note.

Sounds reasonable as a simple, explainable rule. A few quick thoughts to tighten it so "0.8 side-note" behaves well in practice:

- Calibrate the score distribution first. Is 0.8 actually the top ~5% of items, or something else? Run a batch on historical data and inspect percentiles so the threshold matches your intended frequency.

- Make "side-note" delivery conditional on context signals even if it passes 0.8. Example: suppress during calendar Busy / DND, when full-screen video/audio is playing, or while driving. That prevents high-value items from becoming high-annoyance.

- Add temporal throttles so users don't see many 0.8+ items at once:
  - max 1 side-note per X minutes (start with X=30–60).
  - max Y per day (Y=2–4 depending on your product tone).
  - avoid back-to-back topics from the same tag to reduce perceived repetitiveness.

- Use short-term feedback to adapt thresholds per user. If the user dismisses the side-note within T seconds repeatedly, nudge threshold upward for them (e.g., +0.05) or lengthen throttle window.

- Consider a soft tier above 0.95 for stronger presentation (push/sound) versus 0.8–0.95 as subtle. That keeps the strict-threshold idea while allowing graded urgency.

- Expose transparency and controls: show why it was surfaced ("Because you last reviewed X 21 days ago" or "High priority: Project Y") and let the user "snooze topic", "show less like this", or "more like this". That both trains the reranker and reduces annoyance.

If you want, I can:
- Suggest a quick calibration script to compute percentiles and recommended thresholds from your score outputs.
- Draft the UI copy for the side-note explaining why it appeared. Which would be more useful now?
